title: Connection Reuse, Pools, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and Concurrency · Automation and Data Client Systems · Program Defense
summary: A production data release board evaluating a changed source contract must defend independent decisions across fresh cases, including one ambiguous failure and one changed boundary for connection reuse, pools, http/1.1, http/2, http/3, and concurrency, producing connection lifecycle and concurrency plan. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a Python 3.14 urllib and HTTP client layers connection lifecycle and concurrency plan. Learners progress through prediction, e…
objectives: Reuse configured clients and connection pools while separating per-request state from long-lived transport state. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

Explain persistent HTTP/1.1 connections, body completion, idle reuse, pipelining limits, and close behavior. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

Model HTTP/2 streams, field compression, flow control, concurrency limits, and connection-level failure. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""http-clients-python cumulative safe data-client portfolio.

Browser labs use deterministic request and response models only. Live urllib,
DNS, TLS, sockets, concurrency, and load require local Python 3.14 transfer gates.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RequestEvidence:
    method: str
    target: str
    attempt: int

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ResponseEvidence:
    status: int
    body_bytes: int
    valid: bool

print("Python HTTP client portfolio ready")

steps.title: Python Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Concurrency Saturation
steps.instruction:
quiz case — production data release candidate schedules 48 operations across HTTP/1.1 keep-alive, an HTTP/2 connection limited to 6 streams, and optional HTTP/3. Bound queues and memory, reuse clients, finish bodies, and distinguish transport from semantics. Competency probe: Bound concurrent requests, queued work, connections, streams, memory, and downstream pressure from measured capacity. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

Evidence target: Python Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Concurrency Saturation.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Bound concurrent requests, queued work, connections, streams, memory, and downstream pressure from measured capacity. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

At Python Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Concurrency Saturation, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: production data release candidate schedules 24 operations across HTTP/1.1 keep-alive, an HTTP/2 connection limited to 3 streams, and optional HTTP/3. Bound queues and memory, reuse clients, finish bodies, and distinguish transport from semantics. Competency probe: Bound concurrent requests, queued work, connections, streams, memory, and downstream pressure from measured capacity. Demonstrate the result through Python 3.14 urllib, http.client, ssl, typed protocols, and fixture transports.

For Python Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Concurrency Saturation, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Launching one task per item maximizes throughput without affecting reliability. This remains false for Python 3.14 urllib and HTTP client layers.
